fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This is India's largest and most consistent fund for anyone who wants 'set it and forget it'. It automatically shifts between large and mid-cap stocks based on what's doing good right now. It had amazing returns in the past decade and is perfect for a 'just grow' goal.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: You want a simple 2-3 fund portfolio? This is the core for 'just growing'. It basically buys all mid-cap stocks in Nifty in equal amounts and holds them. It's much cheaper to run than a normal fund, and mid-caps have been stars in the last 10 years. Holding them for 28 years gives huge time to recover from bad years.

fund.name: BANK OF INDIA MID & SMALL CAP EQUITY & DEBT FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH
fund.fund_house: Bank of India Investment Managers Private Limited

allocations.rationale: This is your safety net. It mixes stocks with debt to smooth out the wild swings common in small/mid caps. It has monthly auto-SIP, which fits your freelance flow perfectly. More importantly, it's an 'Aggressive Hybrid' fund (hum calls it Balanced Advantage) so it's taxed like an equity fund (12.5% tax on gains) which is much better for you than debt funds under the new regime.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series I Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Under the new income tax regime (which means you pay fixed taxes regardless of income), you can't claim 80C deductions. ELSS funds are now just another equity fund with a 3-year lock-in. Since you prefer minimal hassle and no lock-ins for your 'just grow' goal, I'm leaving this out for now.
Gold is great insurance, but you don't need it for a 28-year horizon. It adds a bit too much risk and is useless for your main goal. Skip it!

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Research Phase. Download the Kuvera or Groww app. It's free, and the interface is simple. Step 2: Lumpsum your ₹1 lakh. Go to the HDFC Flexi Cap Fund and select 'Direct Growth'. Click SIP, enter '100000' as the amount. Once the transaction is done, you are in. Step 3: Monthly SIPs. Check your bank account. On the 25th (or any convenient day), transfer ₹8000 to your mutual fund account. Use the BSIP/Step-up feature to automatically invest in Motilal Oswal and BoI. Step 4: Monitor. Every 6 months, check if the 'Emergency Fund' is set aside (which is a separate savings account, not a fund).
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check your account. Are the top-ups running? If the app crashes (it never does), switch to manual investment.
After 1 year: Check your Motilal Oswal fund. Are you behind Nifty Midcap? No problem. Mid-caps take time.
After 3 years: Rebalance. If you have more than 70% in stocks, take some profit. If less, add more.
After 5 years: Check your lower-risk allocation. If you need to access cash for marriage/travel, move it from here to a Fixed Deposit or Liquid Fund.
At any point: Check if you got a bonus in your freelance work. Invest it in the Motilal fund!
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Rebalance once a year. Check your total portfolio. If stocks are too high (say >70%), sell a bit and buy the safer fund. If stocks are too low (<50%), buy more. This keeps your risk in check. Since you want simplicity, you can ignore this and just keep adding money. Taxes are low on equity (12.5% on gains), so you can wait to sell.
plan.rationale: You are at 27 with 28 years ahead. You are a freelancer, so you want to have enough time to recover from market crashes. We've put 85% (roughly ₹56k/month + ₹6k/month) in funds that track the stock market because that's where you can grow the most over time. The 'safety' fund (14%) uses your small, consistent monthly SIPs to avoid the wild swings of small-mid caps. The emergency fund is separate (liquid/deposit) to protect your freelance cash flow. This is a 'buy and hold' plan. Don't try to time the market! Don't sell in a crash. It just gets you rich over a long time.
risks: Stock markets can drop 30% in a bad year. Your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal. It needs time to recover. You have 28 years! That's plenty of time.

Freelance income means you might not be able to invest every month. That's fine. You can skip SIPs or just invest on the day you get paid. Missed months just mean 'catch-up' months later. It won't ruin your plan.
Inflation can eat away your real returns. Investing in real assets (stocks) is the only way to protect against inflation.
Gold and real estate can be risky. We are skipping them for a simple, long-term plan.
plan.disclaimer: This is for educational/research purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIPs by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: The portfolio scores as moderately passive-to-active (0.45) because while the holdings include passive index funds, the weightage is significant; however, the core Flexi Cap fund has active risk metrics (beta 0.9, tracking error 3.88) suggesting active management, and the Aggressive Hybrid is also active. For passive_instrument_fraction (0.65), the equity sleeve includes one pure index fund (35.83%) and two active funds (HDFC Flexi 50% is active, BoI Hybrid active), yielding ~0.71, slightly offset by the small active exposure of the index fund's actual holdings vs pure cap-weighting nuances, hence 0.65. The cost_emphasis_score (0.4) reflects moderate focus—selecting low-expense index funds and noting tax efficiency, but also prioritizing 'flexibility' and 'set it and forget it' over pure cost minimization. research_vs_cost_score (0.6) balances the absence of deep security research with a modest focus on cost over alpha. time_horizon_alignment_score (0.85) is high, as the plan is built for a 28-year horizon with minimal intervention. turnover_score (0.7) shows low transaction frequency but includes significant tactical rebalancing (e.g., shifting equity from 85% to 30% over time), making it less buy-and-hold than ideal. portfolio_activeness_score (0.45) reflects a mix: the index fund is passive, but the active funds' risk profiles (alpha, tracking error) pull the average toward active management. The overall rationale is neither purely accumulating alpha nor indexing broadly.

pqs.reasoning:
The plan shows strong alignment with the investor's 28-year horizon and freelance income mode, offering SIP flexibility. Tax efficiency is near-optimal: it correctly avoids ELSS for a new_regime investor and uses equity-taxed wrappers. Risk management is good for a 28-year horizon, with JP noting that early years can bear higher volatility.

However, the 'goal alignment' score is kept moderate because the plan ignores the specific '5-year marriage/travel fund' goal by keeping 100% aggressive allocation until Year 10, rather than parking a portion in debt. Internal consistency (0.45) is the weakest dimension due to a major flaw: the 'Monthly SIPs' section describes using ₹8000 total, but the fund schedule explicitly allocates ₹6000 + ₹2000 = ₹8000. The 'Monthly SIPs' section is internally consistent, but the 'rebalancing guidelines' create a contradiction: they state 'If stocks are too high (say >70%)...' while the allocation schedule at Year 1 & 5 already sits at ~86% equity, implying the 70% trigger is only relevant later, yet the text phrasing creates a slight logical disconnect about when the threshold applies. Additionally, the 'safety net' rationale claims the Aggressive Hybrid provides 'monthly auto-SIP' to fit freelance flow, which is an inaccurate description of the fund's feature being the vehicle's investment method, not the fund's auto-SIP capability itself.

Diversification (0.62) is slightly penalized because the portfolio is concentrated in just three active/index funds plus a 'backup' ELSS allocation that is set to 0%, offering less breadth than a 4-5 fund portfolio. Gold (0%) is deemed unnecessary for 28 years, which is a reasonable view but reduces diversification slightly.
